coredata.dc:title: Fe3+-functionalized carbon quantum dots: A facile preparation strategy and detection for ascorbic acid in rat brain microdialysates
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Linbo
Wang, Chao
Luo, Jingxuan
Guo, Quanwei
Liu, Kangyu
Liu, Kun
Zhao, Wenji
Lin, Yuqing
coredata.dc:description: This study reports an Fe3+-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Fe3+-functionalized CQDs) for the highly sensitive and selective detection of ascorbic acid (AA) in rat brain microdialysates based on the specific redox reaction between iron(III) ions and AA. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by one-step pyrolysis of a small organic molecules i.e. tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris). Fe3+ can tightly chelate to the surface of CQDs by the hydroxyl group to form Fe3+-functionalized CQDs while the fluorescence of CQDs can be effectively quenched by Fe3+ via Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The fluorescence of the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs can be sensitively turned on by AA to give an “on–off–on” fluorescence response through the oxidation–reduction between Fe3+ and AA since the produced Fe2+ has much lower chelating ability to CQDs and the fluorescence of CQDs can be restored. This Fe3+-functionalized CQDs based nanoprobe shows high selective and sensitive response in the concentration of AA ranging from 0.1μM to 50μM with the detection limit as lower as 9.1nM, which is lower than other assays. Finally, the proposed fluorescent probe was successfully applied to direct analysis of AA in biological fluids, i.e. rat brain microdialysates, and may pave a new route to the design of effective carbon quantum dots-based fluorescence probes for other bioassay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Iron(III) ion
Ascorbic acid
Brain microdialysate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the Tris-derived CQDs and Fe3+-functionalized CQDs
Detection of AA using Fe3+-functionalized CQDs
Detection of AA using Fe3+-functionalized CQDs
Principles of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs for AA detection
Detection of AA in rat brain microdialysates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Tris-derived CQDs. (b) Size distribution of Tris-derived CQDs.

(A) UV–vis spectra of Tris molecule and Tris-derived CQDs. The inset are the photographs of Tris-derived CQDs under visible light (left) and a UV beam of 365nm (right). (B) Fluorescence excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra of Tris-derived CQDs. (C) FT-IR spectra of the Tris (a) and Tris-derived CQDs (b).

(a) Fluorescence intensities of Tris-derived CQDs with different values of pH (pH from left to right was: 1.0, 4.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 10.0 and 14.0). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Tris-derived CQDs fluorescence quenching while Fe3+ as the quencher. The concentrations of Fe3+ from top to down were: 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 25, 50 and 100mM. (c) Fluorescence intensities of the addition of 10 and 50μM AA into Fe3+-functionalized CQDs solutions with different concentrations of Fe3+ respectively. And the D-value refers to fluorescence restores value between absence and presence of AA in the solutions.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs with difference concentration of AA (from bottom to up: 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 15, 25, 40, 50μM, respectively). The excitation wavelength was locked at 365nm for the fluorescence spectra. The inset in (a) shows the fluorescence spectra of AA concentration range from 0.1 to 5μM. (b) The curve of fluorescence restoration was evaluated with (F 1–F 0)/F 0 vs. AA concentration range from 0.1 to 50μM. The inset in (b) is the linear calibration plot for AA detection ranging from 0.1 to 50μM.

Fluorescence intensity ratios F/F 0 of the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs with different metal irons and biological molecules: blank, K+ (2.4mM), Na+ (128mM), Ca2+ (100μM), Mg2+ (1.5μM), Cu2+ (5μM), Co2+ (2μM), Ni2+ (2μM), Cd2+ (2μM), Zn2+ (5μM), UA (10μM), DA (200nM), DOPAC (10μM), l-Cys (100nM), GSSG (100nM), H2O2 (1μM) and AA (10μM).
EDX spectra of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs. The inset shows the EDX spectra for X-ray energy ranging from 0.0 to 0.65keV.

Fluorescence spectra of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs for AA detection in rat brain microdialysates: Fe3+-functionalized CQDs (a); the successive addition of 0.1mL microdialysates (b), 0.1mL AA solution (c) and one more 0.1mL AA solution (d).
Proposed mechanism of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs probe for AA Sensing.
Comparison of different probes for AA detection.

head.ce:title: Fe3+-functionalized carbon quantum dots: A facile preparation strategy and detection for ascorbic acid in rat brain microdialysates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources Environment and Tourism, Capital Normal University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Resources Environment and Tourism, Capital Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Capital University of Physical Education and Sports, Beijing 100191, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Capital University of Physical Education and Sports

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports an Fe3+-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Fe3+-functionalized CQDs) for the highly sensitive and selective detection of ascorbic acid (AA) in rat brain microdialysates based on the specific redox reaction between iron(III) ions and AA. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by one-step pyrolysis of a small organic molecules i.e. tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris). Fe3+ can tightly chelate to the surface of CQDs by the hydroxyl group to form Fe3+-functionalized CQDs while the fluorescence of CQDs can be effectively quenched by Fe3+ via Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The fluorescence of the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs can be sensitively turned on by AA to give an “on–off–on” fluorescence response through the oxidation–reduction between Fe3+ and AA since the produced Fe2+ has much lower chelating ability to CQDs and the fluorescence of CQDs can be restored. This Fe3+-functionalized CQDs based nanoprobe shows high selective and sensitive response in the concentration of AA ranging from 0.1μM to 50μM with the detection limit as lower as 9.1nM, which is lower than other assays. Finally, the proposed fluorescent probe was successfully applied to direct analysis of AA in biological fluids, i.e. rat brain microdialysates, and may pave a new route to the design of effective carbon quantum dots-based fluorescence probes for other bioassay.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, is one of the basic vitamins in biological systems and plays a vital role in many physiological and pathological processes [1]. AA can prevent human disease as a cofactor, an enzyme complement or a co-substrate in several biochemical reactions [2]. Specifically, AA has antioxidant properties and stabilizes a number of compounds, including vitamin E and folic acid. Iron absorption is also enhanced as it is reduced to the more absorbable ferrous state by AA. A lack of AA can induce oxidative damage to lipids, DNA and proteins; thus, AA has been implicated in many chronic diseases [3]. The detection of AA is of great importance for the early and rapid detection of these diseases.

Many elegant methods for AA detection, including capillary electrophoresis [4], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry [6], enzymatic method [7] and electrochemistry assay [8] have been reported. Although many approaches were well-established, sophisticated instrumentation, tedious sample preparation and separation procedures involved limit their practical applications. To resolve these problems, Mao's group successfully developed electrochemical methods with high selectivity and reproducibility for in vivo/online measurement of AA [9]. Nevertheless, more simple but effective measurement methods for AA in the cerebral systems are still urgently desired. Fluorescence probes providing a better choice of AA detection in living cells, tissues, even animals owing to the unique advantages of high sensitivity, simplicity, easy monitoring and rapid response etc [10]. However, most of the traditional fluorescent probes such as organic chromophores, fluorescent conjugated polymers and semiconductor quantum dots are biologically incompatible since they are poisonous and water-insoluble, and greatly limit the detection of AA in bioassay [11]. Accordingly, the development of a novel fluorescent probe with excellent biocompatibility has attracted increasing attention.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with fluorescence have drawn a wide interest in biological studies since they were discovered by Xu and Sun [12,13]. The advantages of CQDs include their small size, good water-solubility, the potential for large-scale and low-cost synthesis, easy functionalization, and they are both excellent electron donors and excellent electron acceptors. Thus they are being hailed as a new platform for fluorescent biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery and reaction catalysis [14] because of their excellent properties, which include size-tuneable optical properties, high biocompatibility, low-toxicity, chemical inertness and good water-solubility when compared with other quantum dots [15]. Recent reports of applications for CQDs conclude that they can sense metal cations and acid radical anions. For instance, Li, Liu and Chen used a fluorescence “turn-off” probe for Fe3+, Cu2+ and Hg2+ sensing based on chelating reactions or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the surface of CQDs and metal cations, respectively [16–18]. While Lin, Zhao and Du group designed a fluorescence probe for
NO 2 -
,
PO 4
3 -
and I− sensing based on simple complexation reactions [19–21]. Until now, there are relatively few applications for CQDs in biological organic molecular sensing in a physiological environment because of a lack of specific reactions between the CQDs and biomolecules. In the few examples that have been described, the fluorescence “turn-off” probes mainly used FRET as the main principles for dopamine, acetylcholine and glucose biosensing, where the detection of acetylcholine and glucose were based on detection of hydrogen peroxide, the product of enzymatic reactions [22–24].

In this study, we report a Fe3+-functionalized carbon quantum dot (Fe3+-functionalized CQDs) prepared by a simple strategy for the highly sensitive and selective detection of AA in rat brain microdialysates. The detection is based on specific redox reaction between iron(III) ions and AA ( Scheme 1), in which the Tris-derived CQDs are used as the fluorescence units while the Fe3+ ion quenches fluorescence via FRET. The fluorescence of the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs could be sensitively turned on by AA through the oxidation–reduction reaction between Fe3+ and AA, because the Fe2+ product has much lower ability to chelate CQDs (the complex stability constants for a hydroxide-Fe3+ complex is 1.00×1011, and the hydroxide-Fe2+ complex is 3.16×104) and the fluorescence of CQDs can be restored. Furthermore, the Fe3+-functionalized CQD probes show a sensitive response to AA detection in a concentration range from 0.1 to 50μM with a detection limit as low as 9.1nM. This detection limit is much lower than many detection assays for AA and makes it widely applicable. The developed fluorescent probe was successfully applied to the direct analysis of AA in rat brain microdialysates and may find additional potential applications in bioassays.

Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), ascorbic acid (AA), l-cysteine (l-Cys), uric acid (UA), dopamine (DA), 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC), l-Glutathione oxidized (GSSG) and sodium hydroxide were all purchased from Sigma (Steinheim, Germany). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), FeCl3·6H2O, NaCl, MgSO4·2H2O, KCl, CaCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CdSO4·2H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, NaHCO3,Na2SO4 and KH2PO4 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). All reagents are of analytical reagent grade and used as received. And all aqueous solutions were prepared with double-distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Tris-derived CQDs and Fe3+-functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Tris-derived CQDs were synthesized though pyrolysis [25] by heating 1g of Tris at 230°C for 20min using a heating mantle until the color of the liquid changed from colorless to red wine and was then dissolved in ultrapure water. The Tris-derived CQDs were diluted to 120mL (approximately 8.33mg/mL). The pH of the Tris-derived CQDs aqueous solutions was adjusted to 7.0 with 1M HCl solution. Then, the PL quantum yields of the Tris-derived CQDs were studied via UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The Fe3+-functionalized CQDs were prepared by mixing the Tris-derived CQDs and a FeCl3 aqueous solution (10mM) with a volume ratio of 10:1.

Transmission electron micrographs (TEM) images were collected on a JEM-2100F Transmission Electron Microscope (Japan) operated at 200kV. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Tris and Tris-derived CQDs were accomplished by Equinox55spectrophotometer (BRUKER, Germany). Fluorescence and UV–visible absorption spectra were using a 1cm path length quartz cell at room temperature based on using a Fluorescence F-4500 (Hitachi, Japan) and UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. To explore the working principles of this paper, Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) was characterized by FE-SEM (SU8010, Hitachi, Japan), and the methods of chronoamperometry was carried out with a computer-controlled electrochemical analyzer (CHI1030C, Chenhua, China) in a two compartment electrochemical cell with a bare GCE (3mm in diameter) as working electrode, a platinum wire as counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode (KCl-saturated) as reference electrode. All the electrochemistry experiments were also performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA using Fe3+-functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: AA was detected using a 1cm path length quartz cell at room temperature with a fluorescence excitation wavelength of 365nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of the Tris-derived CQDs and Fe3+-functionalized CQDs were taken as a control. Then, 0.1mL of different concentrations of AA was injected into 1.0mL of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs. The concentrations of AA were 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 15, 25, 40 and 50μM. Fluorescence emission spectra were taken after incubating for 5min.

In vivo microdialysis experiments were conducted according to previous reports [26]. Briefly, rats with a guide cannula were anesthetized with chloral hydrate (350mg/kg i.p.). A microdialysis probe (2mm in length; Bioanalytical Systems Inc. (BAS), BAS Carnegie Medicine) was implanted into the brain through the guide cannula and was perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF) (126mM NaCl, 2.4mM KCl, 1.1mM CaCl2, 0.85mM MgCl2, 27.5mM NaHCO3, 0.5mM Na2SO4, 0.5mM KH2PO4, pH 7.0) at a flow rate of 3μL/min driven by a microinjection pump (CMA/100; CMA Microdialysis AB, Stockholm, Sweden). After equilibrating for 100min by continuously perfusing aCSF through the probes for equilibration, 100μL of brain region dialysates were collected for analysis. The AA detection in the microdialysates was performed using the same conditions as the linear detection. The amount of AA in the rat brain microdialysates samples was estimated by using the standard addition method with 5μM AA as the standard.

TEM images show that the Tris-derived CQDs were well-dispersed in the aqueous phase ( Fig. 1a) and have a size distribution ranging from 2.0 to 4.5nm with an average diameter of 3.2nm (Fig. 1b). According to the UV–visible absorption feature of the Tris and the Tris-derived CQDs spectra in Fig. 2A at 280–380nm, the Tris-derived CQDs exhibit a wide absorption band that is different from Tris. Absorption that is typical of an aromatic π system is present and is similar to that of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [27]. This wide band from 250nm to 380nm is unique to Tris-derived CQDs, whereas the Tris solution does not have this absorption. Because of this absorption band, the energy of the surface states of the carbon quantum dots can be excited, producing a strong blue–green fluorescence color as shown in the inset in Fig. 2A [28]. Furthermore, the fluorescence excitation maximum of the Tris-derived CQDs occurs at 365nm, as shown in Fig. 2B, which is consistent with the UV–vis spectrum. The room temperature fluorescence emission peak of the Tris-derived CQDs was locked at 445nm, and the PL quantum yield was 5.84%. Therefore, we successfully synthesized a stable carbon quantum dot.

The FT-IR spectra of the Tris and the Tris-derived CQDs are shown in Fig. 2C. The –NH2 groups are present in Tris (Fig. 2C-a), evidenced by the double bands at ~3352cm−1 and ~3293cm−1 which arise from the N–H out-of-plane stretching, whereas the bands at ~3202cm−1 with ~2074cm−1 are attributed to N–H in-plane stretching. The bands at ~1585cm−1 are assigned to N–H bending vibrations [29]. The broad band in Tris-derived CQDs (Fig. 2C-b) at 3500–3283cm−1 and ~1591cm−1 almost disappears in the Tris spectrum, indicating that the dehydration produced an absence of –NH2 groups in Tris-derived CQDs through the pyrolysis treatment of Tris. The peaks at 2944, 2848, 1464 and 1290cm−1 correspond to C–H vibrations in Tris, whereas two new peaks at 2923 and 1644cm−1 in the Tris-derived CQDs correspond to the C–H and C=C stretching of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [36]. These indicate that the temperature of pyrolysis at 230°C is high enough to break and oxidize the C–C, and the aromatic π system is retained in the carbon quantum dots. Lastly, the peaks at 1397 and 1042cm−1 in Tris and the associated peaks at 1403 and 1037cm−1 in the Tris-derived CQDs are ascribed to the C–OH and CH2–OH groups [30], respectively.

The pH of the CQDs solution can influence the fluorescence intensity because the surface groups of CQDs exist in different forms at different pH values [31]. Previous reports indicate that CQDs were designed as pH sensors [32]. As shown in Fig. 3a, pH of 7.0 was chosen from the possible range of 1.0–11.0, where CQDs displayed the highest fluorescence intensity. The concentration of Fe3+ also influenced the AA detection response. To optimize the concentration of Fe3+, 0.1mL of different concentrations of Fe3+ aqueous solutions was added into 1mL of Tris-derived CQDs and the fluorescence emission spectra at 365nm were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3b, with increasing concentrations of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity was gradually quenched. After that, the same concentrations and volumes of AA were added to all of the solutions and the fluorescence emission spectra excited at 365nm were recorded again. A comparison of the fluorescence recovery value in the absence and presence of AA enables the identification of a suitable Fe3+ concentration for sensitive detection. Two series of AA concentration (50μM and 10μM) were performed in Fig. 3c. In the series with 50μM AA, when the Fe3+ concentration was held at 50mM, the fluorescence recovery value reached a maximum. In the series with 10μM AA, 10mM of Fe3+ concentration reached a maximum. Because 10μM of AA is closer to the real concentration level in biological cells, the concentration of Fe3+ was set to 10mM. With the optimized pH and Fe3+ concentrations, the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs show a sensitive response for detection of AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA using Fe3+-functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the optimized condition, we explored the application of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs as a fluorescence probe for the detection of AA. When different AA concentrations ranging from 0 to 50μM were added into Fe3+-functionalized CQDs, the fluorescence intensities of the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs were increased at 445nm ( Fig. 4a). Fig. 4b demonstrates the relationship between the fluorescence restoration ratio (F 1–F 0)/F 0 (F 1 and F 0 refer to the fluorescence intensities of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs at 445nm in the presence and absence of AA, respectively) with different concentrations of AA. The inset of Fig. 4b shows that the ratio (F 1–F 0)/F 0 has a linear relationship with AA concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 50μM. The linear regression equation is (F 1−F 0)/F 0=0.04242+0.00374C with a correlation coefficient of 0.995, where C is the concentration of AA (μM). The volume ratio between the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs and the AA samples was 10:1, with each AA sample being diluted 11 times. The limitation of detection (LOD) was 9.1nM, which is much lower than most of the previously reported assays for AA detection [11,33–39]. Comparisons between different fluorescence probes for AA detection are presented in Table 1 and suggest that our system exhibits higher sensitivity than most ones.

To explore the selectivity of this fluorescence probe, the fluorescence intensity ratios F/F 0 (F and F 0 refer to the fluorescence intensities of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs at 445nm in the presence and absence of different interference, respectively) of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs were analyzed by adding different metal irons and biomolecules to the system, including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, UA, DA, DOPAC, l-Cys, GSSG, H2O2 and AA. The concentrations of different ions and biological molecules were based on previous reports [40] and were: K+ (2.4mM), Na+ (128mM), Ca2+ (100μM), Mg2+ (1.5μM), Cu2+ (5μM), Co2+ (2μM), Ni2+ (2μM), Cd2+ (2μM), Zn2+ (5μM), UA (10μM), DA (200nM), DOPAC (10μM), l-Cys (100nM), GSSG (100nM) and H2O2 (1μM). The concentration of AA was set to 10μM. Fig. 5 demonstrates that only AA elicits an obvious response from the fluorescence of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs, reflecting the high selectivity of the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs for AA in aqueous solution. The other ions and biological molecules in the system induce fluorescence quenching except for UA, possible because of FRET or weak interactions including chelation [41], hydrogen bonds [10] or etching effects [23]. It further indicates that this method can be used to detect AA in the presence of potential interfering substances in a biological environment or in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principles of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs for AA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The underlying principles were also explored in this study. First, the fluorescence quenching was an effect of iron ions on the CQDs by Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Previous studies report that many heavy metal ions and metal hydroxides can lead to fluorescence quenching, and these principles have been examined elsewhere [16,39]. In this study, the Tris-derived CQDs acted as the fluorescence donor while the Fe3+ ion was the fluorescence acceptor, which makes the fluorescence of the CQDs turn off. EDX spectrum analysis was also performed on the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs. Before EDX spectrum analysis, the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 24h (retained molecular weight: 1000Da, Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China) to purify the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs and remove free unchelated Fe3+. In Fig. 6, Fe is clearly observed together with C, O and N in the EDX spectra, which indicates that the Fe3+ was tightly bound to the Tris-derived CQDs through coordination bonds between the alcoholic hydroxyl on the surface of Tris-derived CQDs and the Fe3+ ion. In this way, the electrons in the excited stage of Tris-derived CQDs can transfer to the half-filled 3d orbits of Fe3+ ions and lead to significant fluorescence quenching [16].

To explain the oxidation–reduction reaction between the AA and the Fe3+ ion in the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs, the chronoamperometry spectra were recorded at +0.5V. At this potential, if reducing substances like AA or Fe3+ are present in the solution, the current in the chronoamperometry will increase. According to Fig. S1 in the supporting information (SI), we can see that the Fe2+ ion is present and that free AA is absent from the system after the reaction of Fe3+-functionalized CQDs and AA. Furthermore, the theoretical stability constant for the complex between hydroxide and Fe3+ is 1.00×1011, whereas that of hydroxide and Fe2+ is lower at 3.16×104, which indicates that the Fe2+ product may separate from the surface of the CQDs after the redox reaction and would turn on the fluorescence.

The Fe3+-functionalized CQDs probe shows a sensitive response to AA detection in the 0.1–50μM range with a detection limit as low as 9.1nM because of a stable yield of Tris-derived CQDs at 5.84% and a special redox reaction between AA and Fe3+. The special redox reaction is used to improve the selectivity of the fluorescence probe when AA is contacted by the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs in aqueous solutions. The enediol group of AA is oxidized to an ortho-dione group of dehydroascorbic acid, and the Fe3+ ion of the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs is reduced to an Fe2+ ion. The results of attempted interference with the experiment indicates that only the AA has a strong response to the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs, which is attributed to the special redox reaction. As a result it improves the ability to sensitively and selectively detect AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in rat brain microdialysates
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the applications of this method, the fluorescence probe was used to detect AA in rat brain microdialysates. Detecting the level of substances in brain microdialysates is important for physiology, pathology and clinics, and many reports use detection assays for neuromolecules such as ascorbate, glucose, lactate and dopamine in rat brain microdialysates [40–42]. In our experiment, an unknown amount of AA in a rat brain microdialysis sample was estimated by using the standard addition method with 5μM AA solution. F 0 and F 1 were the fluorescence intensities of the Fe3+-functionalized CQDs at 445nm in the presence or absence of microdialysates, respectively. Next, 0.1mL of 5μM AA standard solution was added twice, recording each of the fluorescence intensities, F 2 and F 3, respectively. Then, as shown in Fig. 7, fitting the fluorescence restoration value ΔF=Fn −F 0 (n=1, 2, 3) and concentrations of AA in each of the samples for linear relationships, obtained a linear equation of ΔF=66.79+229.88C with a correlation coefficient of 0.999, where C is the concentration of AA (μM) in each sample. Finally, the concentration of AA was calculated to be 3.19μM in the rat brain microdialysates, which is consistent with the previous reports [43].

In summary, we developed an efficient fluorescence approach using Fe3+-functionalized CQDs for the determination AA based on the specific reaction of Fe3+ and AA. The Tris-derived CQDs owns a strong blue–green fluorescence and would be turned off in the presence of Fe3+ due to the FRET, while the fluorescence intensity would recover with the subsequent addition of AA via the redox between Fe3+ and AA, thereby enabling the highly selective and sensitive detection of AA. The fluorescence assay was successfully applied for the detection of AA in rat brain microdialysates samples. We anticipate that this approach involving a facile probe preparation strategy may provide new insights for monitoring AA in biological and analytical sciences.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375088), Scientific Research Project of Beijing Educational Committee (KM201410028006), Youth Talent Project of the Beijing Municipal Commission of Education (CIT&TCD201504072), Scientific Research Base Development Program of the Beijing Municipal Commission of Education and the 2013 Program of Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars of Beijing Municipality.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2015.08.003.
